questions.question: How are ions confined in a Paul trap?

options.text: Oscillating electric fields creating a pseudopotential

questions.explanation: Paul traps use oscillating RF electric fields that create a time-averaged pseudopotential, confining charged ions in 3D space.

questions.question: Why is ¹⁷¹Yb⁺ a popular choice for trapped ion qubits?

options.text: It has clock transition states insensitive to magnetic fields

questions.explanation: ¹⁷¹Yb⁺ has 'clock state' hyperfine transitions that are first-order insensitive to magnetic field fluctuations, enabling very long coherence times.

questions.question: How are trapped ion qubits read out?
questions.explanation: A detection laser causes one qubit state to fluoresce brightly (scattering many photons) while the other remains dark, allowing state discrimination.

questions.question: What mediates two-qubit gates between trapped ions?
questions.explanation: Two-qubit gates use the shared motional modes of the ion chain. Lasers couple the internal states to motion, enabling state-dependent forces that create entanglement.

questions.question: What is a key advantage of trapped ions over superconducting qubits?
questions.explanation: Trapped ions in a chain share motional modes, allowing any pair to interact directly (all-to-all connectivity) without needing SWAP gates.

questions.question: What is the QCCD architecture for scaling trapped ions?

options.text: Shuttling ions between different trap zones

questions.explanation: QCCD (Quantum CCD) involves segmented traps where ions are physically shuttled between zones for computation, memory, and communication.

questions.question: Trapped ion two-qubit gates are typically faster than superconducting two-qubit gates.
questions.explanation: False. Trapped ion gates typically take 50-200 μs, while superconducting gates take ~30-60 ns - about 1000× faster. However, ion gates have higher fidelity.
